title: W1 D Suggested Exercises
body:
Suggested Exercises for Week 1 Juan Carlos Carbajal

ECON 3202 — Term 1, 2026 UNSW Sydney

Problem 1. Let n ∈ N be an arbitrary odd number. Show that it can be written as the difference between the square of two positive integers.

Problem 2. Prove the following.

Proposition 1

For all n ∈ N, one has that n ( n + 1) 1 + 2 + ... + n = .

Problem 3. Prove the following lemma.

Lemma 1 Let S and T be any two sets.

(a) S = T if and only if S ⊆ T and T ⊆ S;

(b) If T is finite and S ⊆ T, then |S| ≤ | T |;

Note that the finiteness of T in part (b) is important. If T is infinite, it can contain a strict subset with the same number of elements. Can you think of an example?

Problem 4. Prove that the distributive laws hold.

Lemma 2 Let X, Y, Z be arbitrary sets. Then:

(a) X ∪ (Y ∩ Z ) = ( X ∪ Y ) ∩ ( X ∪ Z );

(b) X ∩ (Y ∪ Z ) = ( X ∩ Y ) ∪ ( X ∩ Z ).

Problem 5. This is an economy with n perfectly divisible goods. For any vector of prices p ≥ 0 and wealth level w ≥ 0, the consumer budget set is

B( p, w) = { x ∈ R n+ | p · x ≤ w}.

• At time t = 1, the consumer has wealth w1 > 0 and the price vector is p1 = ( p11 , . . . , p1n ), where p1i > 0 for each good i = 1, . . . , n.

• At time t = 2, the consumer wealth increases to w2 > w1 > 0, but the price vector is unchanged, so p2 = p1 ; i.e., p2i = p1i for each good i = 1, . . . , n.

• At time t = 3, the consumer wealth remains the same (w3 = w2 ), and so does the price of every good but good 1; i.e., p3i = p2i > 0 for each i = 2, 3, . . . , n. At this time, however, good 1 becomes more affordable: 0 < p31 < p21 .

• At time t = 4, wealth remains as in the previous period, and so does every price but the price of good 2. This good has become less affordable: 0 < p32 < p42 .

(a) Show that B( p1 , w1 ) ⊆ B( p2 , w2 ). In fact, show that the set inclusion is strict: there is at least one consumption bundle x in B( p2 , w2 ) that is not in B( p1 , w1 ). (b) Show that B( p2 , w2 ) ⊆ B( p3 , w3 ). In fact, show that the set inclusion is strict: there is at least one consumption bundle y in B( p3 , w3 ) that is not in B( p2 , w2 ). Note that, in this case, the bundle y cannot have zero consumption of good 1. (c) What can be say about B( p4 , w4 ) with respect to B( p3 , w3 )?

Problem 6. Consider two vectors x = ( x1 , . . . , xn ) and y = (y1 , . . . , yn ) in R n . Say that x is weakly greater than y, written x ≥ y, if and only if xi ≥ yi for all i = 1, . . . , n. Say that x is strictly greater than y, written x > y, if and only if xi > yi for all i = 1, . . . , n.

(a) Show for any x, y and z in R n , x ≥ y and y ≥ z implies x ≥ z. In words, the weakly greater than relation is transitive. (b) Show for any x, y and z in R n , x > y and y > z implies x > z. (c) Is is true that if x ≥ y and y > z, then x > z?

Problem 7. Let a, b ∈ R be any two real numbers. Show that the following holds:

| a | − | b | ≤ | a ± b | ≤ | a | + | b |.

Hint: We have shown the following during lectures, which you can take for granted:

| x | ≤ y ⇐⇒ − y ≤ x ≤ y and − | x | ≤ x ≤ | x |.

Using these inequalities, show first that | a + b| ≤ | a| + |b| and then that | a| − |b| ≤ | a − b|. The remaining inequalities, | a| − |b| ≤ | a + b| and | a − b| ≤ | a| + |b| follow easily.
